data.title: The Great Rotation Has Started — and Maybe Ended. What to Do Next. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Jack Hough

Is this a stock market rotation or a county fair Tilt-a-Whirl? I'm regretting that corn dog either way.

Metals are soaring. Crypto has crashed. Artificial-intelligence stocks are down, but so are perceived AI victims, so either a) the technology is overpriced but not overhyped, or b) we should think about powering the stock market off and then on again.

I'd call this a sector rotation, if boring were a sector. A screen for this year's top-performing S&P 500 index members brings up a who's who of wait, what? Bunge Global, a soybean squeezer, is up 37%; Dow, which makes polyethylene, the most exciting part of milk jugs, 35%; Smurfit Westrock, a big name in cardboard boxes, 34%; Church & Dwight, baking soda, 22%; and Lamb Weston, frozen french fries, 17%. What I wouldn't give for a fingernail clipper pure play with a decent dividend.

It's important to precisely define and label this market shift — how else can I claim to have seen it coming all along, while waffling about what happens next? The technical strategists at J.P. Morgan call it a pro-cyclical rotation, meaning that it's driven by economically sensitive stocks. Fair enough: There goes Deere, up 42% this year; Caterpillar, 33%; and Stanley Black & Decker, 21%. In the U.S. in January, a survey-based leading indicator called the Purchasing Managers' Index, or PMI, ticked up to a level that indicates expanding manufacturing for the first time in a year.

That might just be postholiday reordering. Also, last year, U.S. manufacturing shed 68,000 jobs. So until I see more data, I'm clapping for the new PMI reading, but not making noise, like at a high school basketball game when they announce the other team's starting five. Anyhow, JPM estimates that economically sensitive stocks have already risen to levels that anticipate much higher global PMI levels, so if you sneezed you might have missed the rally, or as they put it, "we think it is unwise to press pro-cyclical trades at current levels."

Barclays sees a temporary shuffling of sector and factor leadership. For sectors, tech and financials fell off, while staples, energy, and materials soared. Staples explains the rally in milk jugs and french fries. In energy and materials, Exxon Mobil and gold miner Newmont are both up about 25% this year. For Exxon, that's a change, and for Newmont, a continuation — it has more than tripled over two years.

For factors, value has beaten growth, small companies have raced ahead of large, and dividends have shined. Vanguard Value, an exchange-traded fund, has returned 8% year to date, beating its growth counterpart by 13 points. The iShares Core S&P Small Cap ETF has returned 9%, and the Schwab US Dividend Equity, 15%. By the way, overseas markets have continued last year's comeback. Vanguard Total International Stock ETF has returned 9% this year, while the S&P 500 is flat.

If I had to sum up recent market action with a film title, I might pick the 2022 absurdist award-winner Everything Everywhere All at Once . Investors have read for years that AI stocks look stretched, and that value stocks, or small-caps, or dividends, or overseas markets are due. They've suddenly decided that all of it sounds about right, and must now be addressed simultaneously and immediately, in an act of violent prudence. By the way, I read that when that movie opened in Hong Kong, the name translated to: Weird Woman Warrior [expletive]s Around and Saves the Universe. That one doesn't seem as applicable to the stock market, but let's see what the rest of February brings.

As for what to do now, some observations: First, flat isn't so bad for a stock index having an identity crisis. For S&P 500 fundholders, of whom there are many, the stuff that's working is offsetting the stuff that's not, at least for now. Second, Barclays notes that the value rally has been driven by expanding valuations, not better earnings growth, and that for small-caps, the trend in earnings revisions has weakened. Meanwhile, economist Ed Yardeni recently pointed out that the so-called Magnificent Seven tech stocks were down to 25.8 times forward earnings from a September 2020 peak of 38.1 times. That compares with 21.8 times recently for the broader S&P 500. The Mag 7 premium is "arguably justified," in Yardeni's view, given that the group is expected to grow earnings by 22.8% in the year ahead, versus 12.9% for the Mag-less S&P 493.

I wonder if that makes it late to pile into value stocks and small-caps. The risk is that the weaker ones that have been pulled up with the group suddenly reverse course — or reverse Coors, you might say. Brewer Molson Coors, which has lost shareholders money for a decade, was up 16% year to date through Feb. 12. But just as shareholders were humming the opening bars to "Rocky Mountain High," the company predicted a sharp drop in this year's profit, and the stock gave back nearly all of its gains. Meanwhile, Vanguard Value goes for 17.2 times this year's projected earnings, which is a discount to the S&P 500's current valuation, but a premium to its historical one.

For investors who wish to complement their S&P 500 funds and who lack international diversification, adding some now still seems a safe bet. That aforementioned Vanguard international ETF, ticker VXUS, doubles as a value fund, at 15 times this year's earnings, and triples as a currency hedge, should the dollar continue the past year's slide.

Finally, I remain a dividend fetishist, even if polite financial society frowns upon my kink. The Schwab ETF previously mentioned, SCHD, still yields 3.4%, which is triple what the S&P 500 pays. It, too, is decidedly tilted toward value, trading at 16.1 times earnings. Plump quarterly payments are relatively scarce, so the fund's top holdings cover mostly familiar ground: oil, soda, fighter jets, pills, cigarettes, and for those with the moral flexibility for it, phone service.

Write to Jack Hough at jack.hough@barrons.com. Follow him on X and subscribe to his Barron's Streetwise podcast.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Sylvamo Corp SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Sylvamo Corporation, a leading global producer of uncoated freesheet paper and market pulp, has released its 2025 10-K report, detailing the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in a competitive and evolving market. The report provides a comprehensive overview of Sylvamo's activities across its key geographical segments: Europe, Latin America, and North America.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Sales: $3,351 million, reflecting a decrease from $3,773 million in 2024 due to lower volumes and unfavorable price and mix in Europe and Latin America.
  • Net Income: $132 million, a significant decrease from $302 million in 2024, impacted by challenging industry conditions and higher input costs.
  • Diluted EPS: $3.24, down from $7.18 in 2024, reflecting the decrease in net income.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: Sylvamo operates through three main geographical segments: Europe, Latin America, and North America, focusing on the manufacture and sale of uncoated freesheet paper and market pulp.
  • Geographical Performance - Europe: The Europe segment experienced a decrease in sales by $60 million in 2025 compared to 2024, primarily due to lower sales prices and mix, as well as lower volumes. The segment faced higher planned maintenance outages and increased operating and input costs.
  • Geographical Performance - Latin America: The Latin America segment saw a $70 million decrease in sales in 2025 compared to 2024, driven by lower sales prices, mix, and volumes, along with unfavorable foreign exchange impacts. Despite these challenges, the segment maintained strong brand recognition with products like Chamex and Chamequinho.
  • Geographical Performance - North America: The North America segment reported a $275 million decrease in sales in 2025 compared to 2024, largely due to the termination of the Georgetown mill offtake agreement. However, the segment benefited from higher sales prices and mix, and lower unabsorbed costs due to economic downtime.
  • New Product Launches: In 2025, Sylvamo invested in finished roll production capabilities at the Saillat mill in France to enhance its product mix and expand its business in graphic and high-speed inkjet printing papers under the brand Berga.
  • New Production Launches: The acquisition of the Nymölla mill in Sweden in January 2023 added significant capacity to the Europe segment, with the mill capable of producing approximately 500,000 short tons of uncoated freesheet on two paper machines.
  • Future Outlook: Looking ahead to 2026, Sylvamo anticipates a transition year for North America due to short-term capacity constraints with the Riverdale supply agreement exit and investments at the Eastover mill. The company is prioritizing strategic projects with the fastest payback to enhance efficiency and cash conversion potential in 2027 and beyond.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Reinvestment: In 2025, Sylvamo reinvested $224 million across its manufacturing network and Brazil forestlands to strengthen its low-cost position. Investments were made in the Europe segment to improve product mix and enhance business in graphic and high-speed inkjet printing papers.
  • Capital Management: The company returned $155 million in cash to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. It paid $73 million in dividends and repurchased $82 million worth of shares. The company also managed its debt by making payments on various outstanding principal debt balances, including Term Loan A, the AR Securitization, and the Revolving Credit Facility. Additionally, the company drew $178 million on its Revolving Credit Facility and $47 million on the AR Securitization.
  • Future Outlook: For 2026, the company plans to prioritize strategic projects with the fastest payback to achieve lower costs, higher efficiency, and stronger cash conversion potential by 2027 and beyond. The company aims to maintain a strong financial position to support reinvestment in its business and increase future earnings and cash flow.

Challenges and Risks

  • Market and Competitive Risks: Sylvamo operates in a highly competitive environment with significant exposure to market fluctuations and competitive pressures. The secular decline in demand for paper products due to digital alternatives could impact future revenue and growth opportunities.
  • Regulatory and Environmental Risks: The company is subject to extensive environmental and regulatory compliance requirements, which could result in significant costs and operational constraints. Evolving climate-related regulations and trade protection measures could affect competitive positioning.
  • Operational Risks: Sylvamo faces operational risks from potential disruptions at manufacturing facilities and in transportation logistics, which could increase costs and impair operations. The company is also addressing labor market challenges by focusing on attracting and retaining skilled employees.
  • Economic and Financial Risks: Global inflation and potential economic recessions could increase operating costs and reduce demand for products. The company is also exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in foreign currency exchange rates and interest rate risks due to its indebtedness.
  • Cybersecurity Risks: Cybersecurity threats and the integration of AI into systems pose additional risks to data security and operational integrity. Management is monitoring these risks and implementing strategies to mitigate their impact.

SEC Filing: Sylvamo Corp [ SLVM ] - 10-K - Feb. 20, 2026

data.title: HANOVER INSURANCE GROUP, INC. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
The Hanover Insurance Group, Inc., a prominent player in the property and casualty insurance market, has released its 2025 Form 10-K report. The report highlights the company's robust financial performance, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in a competitive and regulated industry. The Hanover Insurance Group operates through four main segments: Core Commercial, Specialty, Personal Lines, and Other, providing a wide range of insurance products to small and middle market businesses as well as individual customers.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenues: $6,594.4 million, reflecting a year-over-year increase driven by higher premiums and net investment income.
  • Net Income: $662.5 million, a significant increase from the previous year, indicating improved profitability.
  • Net Income Per Share: $18.50, showcasing strong earnings growth per share compared to prior periods.
  • Diluted EPS: $18.16, highlighting the company's ability to generate earnings on a per-share basis after accounting for potential dilution.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The Hanover Insurance Group operates through four main segments: Core Commercial, Specialty, Personal Lines, and Other. Core Commercial generated $2.3 billion in net premiums written, Specialty $1.4 billion, and Personal Lines $2.6 billion for the year ended December 31, 2025.
  • Core Commercial Segment: This segment focuses on small and middle market businesses, offering products like commercial multiple peril, commercial automobile, and workers' compensation. It generated 36.0% of the company's net premiums written in 2025.
  • Specialty Segment: The Specialty segment offers products for small to mid-sized businesses, including professional and executive lines, marine, and specialty property & casualty. It accounted for 22.8% of net premiums written in 2025.
  • Personal Lines Segment: This segment provides personal automobile and homeowners insurance, contributing 41.2% of net premiums written in 2025. The strategy focuses on account-oriented business through independent agents.
  • Geographical Performance: The company wrote 18.7% of its Core Commercial, Specialty, and Personal Lines business in Michigan and 8.0% in Massachusetts in 2025. Efforts are ongoing to diversify beyond these core states.
  • Claims Management: The company emphasizes efficient, timely, and fair claim settlements, utilizing experienced claims adjusters and technology to enhance claims processing and customer service.
  • Catastrophe Management: The Hanover Insurance Group manages catastrophe risks through underwriting procedures, geographic exposure management, and reinsurance. The company has a structured catastrophe reinsurance program to mitigate potential losses.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue expanding its market reach through differentiated product offerings and selective distribution, with a focus on improving underwriting profitability and geographic diversification.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Capital Structure Enhancement: The Hanover Insurance Group issued $500 million in senior debentures maturing in 2035 to enhance its capital structure and used part of the proceeds to repay $61.8 million of maturing senior debentures. The company also maintains a $150 million credit agreement to ensure liquidity and support strategic initiatives.
  • Shareholder Value: The company repurchased $130.1 million of its own shares, reflecting a commitment to returning value to shareholders. It also paid $132.4 million in dividends, demonstrating a consistent dividend policy. The company increased its additional paid-in capital by $39.9 million through stock-based awards.
  • Future Outlook: The Hanover Insurance Group plans to continue managing its capital structure prudently, focusing on maintaining liquidity and financial flexibility. The company aims to leverage its strong capital position to support growth opportunities and shareholder returns, while adhering to its strategic debt management and equity repurchase programs.

Challenges and Risks

  • Market Risks: Fluctuations in interest rates, inflationary pressures, and economic downturns can adversely affect investment returns and demand for insurance products. The company is also exposed to risks from geographical concentration, particularly in states like Michigan, Massachusetts, California, and Texas.
  • Operational Risks: The effectiveness of pricing models and risk management strategies is crucial. If these models differ materially from actual results, profitability may be adversely affected. The company also faces challenges in managing reserves and claims, especially in long-tail liability lines and emerging risks like cyber threats.
  • Regulatory Risks: The insurance industry is heavily regulated, and changes in regulation can impact profitability. The company is subject to state and federal regulations, including those related to Michigan PIP Reform, which introduces uncertainties in the personal automobile lines.
  • Weather and Catastrophe Risks: The unpredictability of weather events and catastrophes, exacerbated by climate change, poses a significant risk. The company’s ability to predict and manage these risks is limited, and reinsurance may not always provide adequate coverage.
  • Reinsurance Risks: The availability and cost of reinsurance are subject to market conditions and catastrophic events. The company also faces counterparty risks with reinsurers.
  • Technology and Cybersecurity Risks: The company relies on information technology systems and third-party vendors, which are vulnerable to cyberattacks and operational failures. The rapid evolution of technology, including AI, presents both opportunities and risks.

SEC Filing: HANOVER INSURANCE GROUP, INC. [ THG ] - 10-K - Feb. 20, 2026

data.title: Trump adds a 10% tariff on all imports
data.description:
The US President lashed out at the justices, calling them a 'disgrace to the nation' and blamed them for being swayed by 'foreign interests and small political movements'.

Trump claimed that at least three statutes, including The Trade Expansion Act 1962, allow him to charge higher tariffs, if he needed to. And, so he did.

All imports into the US will now face a global tariff of 10% over and above the current rates applicable. "I don't need to ask Congress to take additional action on tariffs," he added.

Emerging MarketCurrent US Tariffs
South Africa30%
Thailand19%
Philippines19%
Malaysia19%
Mexico25%
China47.5%
South Korea25%
Brazil50%
Indonesia19%
India18%

The article will be updated with more details soon.

data.title: CORRECTION FROM SOURCE: Evolve Plans to Launch the Evolve All-in-One UltraYield ETF
data.description:
Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - February 20, 2026) - Evolve Funds Group Inc. ("Evolve" or the "Manager") is pleased to announce that it has filed a preliminary prospectus with the Canadian securities regulators for plans to list the Evolve All-in-One UltraYield ETF ("EASY" or the "Evolve Fund") on the Toronto Stock Exchange. EASY aims to offer investors modestly levered exposure to a portfolio of leading global companies with a covered call strategy and cash distributions paid twice per month.

"We are pleased to announce the filing of a preliminary prospectus for EASY, our All-In-One UltraYield ETF," said Raj Lala, President and CEO at Evolve. "EASY is built as a simple, one-ticket solution that provides diversified global equity exposure while aiming to pay distributions to investors twice per month."

EASY seeks to provide attractive income and long-term capital appreciation by investing in a portfolio of leading global equity securities that have the potential to generate significant option premiums. To enhance yield, as well as to mitigate risk and reduce volatility, EASY will employ a covered call option writing program at the discretion of the Manager. The level of covered call option writing may vary based on market volatility and other factors.

EASY will use modest leverage in order to seek to achieve its investment objective. Leverage will be created through the use of cash borrowings and/or Derivatives, or as otherwise permitted under applicable securities legislation.

Cash distributions, if any, on EASY will be paid at least semi-monthly, with the possibility of more frequent distributions at the Manager's discretion.

About Evolve Funds Group Inc.

With over $8 billion in assets under management, Evolve specializes in bringing innovative ETFs to Canadian investors. Evolve's suite of ETFs provide investors with access to: (i) index-based income strategies; (ii) long term investment themes; and (iii) some of the world's leading investment managers. Established by a team of industry veterans with a demonstrated ability to succeed, Evolve creates investment products that make a difference. For more information, please visit www.evolveetfs.com.

Join us on social media: Twitter | LinkedIn | Facebook | Youtube

Evolve Funds Group Inc. is the investment fund manager and portfolio manager. Evolve All-in-One UltraYield ETF ("EASY") will be offered by Evolve Funds Group Inc., and distributed through authorized dealers.

Leverage increases risk.

A preliminary prospectus containing important information has been filed with the securities commissions or similar authorities in all provinces and territories of Canada. The preliminary prospectus is still subject to completion or amendment. A copy is available on SEDAR+ (www.sedarplus.ca). There will be no sale or acceptance of an offer to buy the securities until receipt for the final prospectus has been issued by the relevant securities commissions. For more information, visit www.evolveetfs.com.

CONTACT INFORMATION

info@evolveetfs.com

t. 416.214.4884

tf. 1.844.370.4884

MEDIA CONTACT

Keith Crone

kcrone@evolveetfs.com

416.966.8716

data.title: Trump Faults Supreme Court for Timing of Decision — WSJ
data.description:
By Victoria Albert

President Trump blamed the Supreme Court for taking too long to issue its decision, saying it contributed to uncertainty for the country.

"We waited months, and that gave uncertainty," Trump said. "Now we have certainty, and I think you're going to see the country get much stronger because of it."

The Supreme Court heard oral arguments on the case in November.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Ajinomoto Recalls a Trader Joe's Chicken-Fried-Rice Item Due to Glass Complaints
data.description:
By Josh Beckerman

Ajinomoto Foods North America recalled a Trader Joe's chicken-fried-rice product due to the possible presence of glass, according to the Agriculture Department's Food Safety and Inspection Service.

The company notified FSIS of four consumer complaints regarding glass. There have been no confirmed reports of injury, FSIS said.

Ajinomoto Foods, part of Japan-based Ajinomoto Co., recalled about 3.37 million pounds of frozen chicken-fried-rice products overall. This includes an Ajinomoto-brand yakitori-chicken product sold only in Canada.

The products subject to recall bear establishment number P-18356 inside the USDA mark of inspection. The Trader Joe's product has best-by dates of 9/8/2026 through 11/17/2026. The yakitori-chicken item has best-before dates of 26 SE 09 through 26 NO 12.

Ajinomoto Foods North America's other consumer brands include Jose Ole, Ling Ling and Tai Pei. It also has multiple foodservice brands.

Write to Josh Beckerman at josh.beckerman@wsj.com

The works will involve building 69 km of new motorway sections, and modernising 28 km of existing sections on the RN154 national highway.

data.title: Massachusetts Advisory Practice Ordered to Pay $150,000 Over Annuity Disclosures—and Show Ruling to Clients — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Kenneth Corbin

A Massachusetts investment advisor and his firm have been ordered to pay $150,000 after a jury found them liable for a violation of the Investment Advisers Act relating to alleged conflicts of interest tied to client investments in annuities. The jury had cleared the defendants of two other charges.

In addition to the monetary penalty, the court also ordered the advisor, Jeffrey Cutter, to present a copy of the judgment to all his current clients and any new clients his firm onboards in the next five years.

In March 2023, the Securities and Exchange Commission announced charges against Jeffrey Cutter and his firm Cutter Financial Group, based in the Cape Cod town of Falmouth, Mass.

The SEC alleged that Cutter had garnered more than $9.3 million in commissions by steering clients toward annuities without disclosing his financial incentive to favor those products over viable alternatives. The commission further alleged that Cutter churned those positions to enhance his commissions and received more than $1 million in undisclosed payments from marketing companies for promoting certain annuities.

Cutter referred a request for comment to his lawyer, Ian Roffman, who was out of the office on Friday and didn't immediately respond to a request for comment. In 2023, when the SEC brought its charges, Roffman told Barron's Advisor that the SEC was "flat-out wrong on the facts and the law," that Cutter had made all necessary disclosures, and that his clients had benefited from his strategy. "No one has lost money," he said at the time.

Last April, a jury held that Cutter and his firm had violated the less-serious antifraud provision of the Advisers Act, which can imply negligence, but cleared him of the charge that he acted knowingly or with intent.

The SEC had asked the court to order Cutter to pay disgorgement of the commissions that he had received from 580 annuity sales as well as interest and a civil penalty. The jury only awarded the civil penalty, directing Cutter to pay $100,000 and his firm to pay $50,000.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Ardelyx Shares Fall After Missing Profit Expectations
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Ardelyx shares fell after the company missed analysts' expectations that it would post a profit.

The stock fell 13% to $5.89 Friday. Shares are still up 2% over the past 12 months.

The biopharmaceutical company recorded a loss of $407,000 in the fourth quarter, which came out to be breaking even on a per-share basis.

Analysts polled by FactSet had been expecting net income of $4,500, or one cent a share.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: US Stocks Mixed in Afternoon Trading
data.description:
US stocks rose in volatile trading on Friday afternoon after the Supreme Court struck down President Trump's emergency tariffs, sparking a relief rally across trade-sensitive sectors.

The S&P 500 gained 0.6% and the Nasdaq climbed 0.8% while the Dow added 0.2% as the court ruled the administration exceeded its authority.

Amazon jumped 2% on the removal of import cost headwinds and Alphabet rose nearly 4% as the decision eased fears of a major inflationary catalyst. The rally pushed past a downbeat GDP report showing the economy grew just 1.4% in Q4, far below estimates.

Markets also navigated sticky inflation data with core PCE holding at 3%, keeping the Fed on a data-dependent path.

While industrials benefited from potential tariff refunds, gold miner Newmont fell 4% on lower production guidance.

Despite the volatility the Nasdaq is set to snap a five-week losing streak as the courts rebuke provides a temporary green light for equity bulls.

data.title: Trump Says He Is 'Deeply Disappointed' by Tariff Ruling — Market Talk
data.description: President Trump says the Supreme Court's tariff ruling is deeply disappointing. "And I'm ashamed of certain members of the Court--absolutely ashamed--for not having the courage to do what's right for our country," he adds. Trump continues to thank the three dissenting justices--Clarence Thomas, Samuel Alito and Brett Kavanaugh--for their strength, wisdom and love of the U.S. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: John Turturro embraces analogue as ageing thief in 'Only Living Pickpocket in New York'
data.description:
Key points:
  • Film features pickpocket struggling with technological change
  • Premiered at Sundance, now in Berlin's Special Gala section
  • Director Noah Segan's second feature film

By Miranda Murray and Swantje Stein

Quintessential New York actor John Turturro said on Friday that his character's commitment to analogue was what had initially drawn him to his new film "The Only Living Pickpocket in New York."

"I'm not a completely analogue person, but I do miss some of that, especially the interactions that happen among people when we're all not looking at our phones," the actor known for his roles in the Coen brothers' classics "Barton Fink" and "The Big Lebowski" told journalists at the Berlin Film Festival.

He added that "it's always interesting when you have characters who actually do things physically" and interact with the world around them - in this case, on location in New York.

CHANGE IS THE 'ONE CONSTANT'

Experienced New York City pickpocket Harry, played by Turturro, shows no sign of slowing down when he targets an ostentatiously wealthy Gen Z high roller, played by "After the Hunt" actor Will Price, while he's parking his car.

Steve Buscemi, Giancarlo Esposito, Victoria Moroles and Jamie Lee Curtis also star as memorable side characters.

Part of the loot is a silver wallet card with a USB attachment with a far higher worth than it appears to the thief, who does not know how to use a cellphone or a computer, and that his mob-connected target will do anything to get back.

"There's only one constant in life, and that's change, radical change," said Turturro, who received a Primetime Emmy award in 2004 for his work in the TV series "Monk."

"You have to sort of adapt to that, and this is a story about a person who is struggling to adapt," he said.

The film, director Noah Segan's second feature after "Blood Relatives," is being shown in the Berlin Film Festival's Special Gala section for high-profile movies after initially premiering at the Sundance Film Festival last month to warm reviews.

data.title: Trump Slams US Supreme Court Tariff Decision, Calls It ‘Deeply Disappointing’
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Friday that the U.S. Supreme Court’s ruling against his trade tariffs is “deeply disappointing.”

In a press briefing, Trump said that he is “ashamed of certain members of the court” for not having the courage to do what’s right for the United States. He said that this was an important case for him as a “symbol of economic national security.”

Earlier on Friday, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled against Trump’s tariffs in a 6-3 decision, saying that the president exceeded his authority to use the emergency-powers law.

Trump also said that after the ruling, foreign countries “that have been ripping us off for years are ecstatic,” but warned that the celebration would not go on for too long.

He also thanked and congratulated Justice Clarence Thomas, Justice Samuel Alito, and Justice Brett Kavanaugh, who dissented in the vote, adding that there was no way to argue against their case.

Get updates to this developing story directly on Stocktwits.

data.title: President Trump Calls Democrats on Supreme Court a Disgrace — Market Talk
data.description: President Trump says Democrats on the Supreme Court are "frankly, a disgrace to our nation." "They're against anything that makes America strong, healthy and great again," Trump says after the court ruled his global tariffs are illegal. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Trump Reportedly Plans New Tariffs On Countries In Response To Court’s Order
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump is reportedly planning to invoke new trade authorities in response to the Supreme Court’s ruling overturning his sweeping tariffs, by potentially including a new, across-the-board tariff on U.S. trading partners.

The Trump administration has been preparing for months for the possibility that the U.S. Supreme court would rule against the president and developed contingency plans as a result, according to a report from New York Times that cited people familiar with the matter. 

The U.S. Supreme Court on Friday struck down President Donald Trump’s tariffs in a 6-3 decision.

Get updates to this developing story directly on Stocktwits.
